title: Low and stable inflation
summary: Low and stable inflation is one of the central macroeconomic objectives. This lesson defines inflation, disinflation and deflation; shows how inflation is measured with a weighted Consumer Price Index (CPI); works through calculating a price index and an inflation rate; examines the limitations of the CPI; analyses the causes of inflation (demand-pull and cost-push) using AD/AS; and evaluates the consequences of high inflation and of deflation, and why price stability matters.
learningObjectives: Define inflation, disinflation and deflation, and distinguish clearly between them.
Explain how inflation is measured using a weighted Consumer Price Index (CPI), and calculate a price index and the rate of inflation.
Evaluate the limitations of the CPI as a measure of the cost of living.
Analyse the causes of inflation — demand-pull and cost-push — using AD/AS diagrams.
Analyse the consequences of high inflation and of deflation, and explain why price stability is a macroeconomic objective.
levelNote: Common to SL and HL at this level.

simpleExplanation.title: Keeping money's value steady
simpleExplanation.summary: Inflation is when the general price level keeps rising, so each unit of money buys less over time. Governments and central banks want inflation to be low AND stable — usually around 2% — because both fast-rising prices and falling prices do real economic damage.
simpleExplanation.analogy: Think of money as a ruler used to measure value. If the ruler keeps shrinking (inflation), every price tag, wage and contract becomes harder to trust, and planning gets messy. If the ruler suddenly grows (deflation), people postpone spending and the economy stalls. A ruler that changes length slowly and predictably lets everyone plan with confidence.
steps: Learn the three terms: inflation (the price level is rising), disinflation (it is still rising but more slowly) and deflation (the price level is actually falling).
Understand measurement: a Consumer Price Index tracks the price of a weighted basket of goods a typical household buys, and the inflation rate is the percentage change in that index.
Diagnose the cause: demand-pull inflation comes from rising aggregate demand; cost-push inflation comes from rising costs that shift short-run aggregate supply left.

Weigh the consequences: high inflation erodes purchasing power and creates uncertainty, while deflation can trap an economy in falling demand and rising real debt — which is why price stability is a policy goal.

flashcards.back: A sustained increase in the general (average) price level of an economy over time, which reduces the purchasing power of money.
A fall in the RATE of inflation. Prices are still rising, just more slowly — e.g. inflation slowing from 5% to 3%. The price level continues to go up.
A sustained decrease in the general price level, i.e. a negative inflation rate. Prices are actually falling, not just rising more slowly.
A weighted index measuring the average change over time in the prices of a basket of goods and services bought by a typical household. It is the most common measure of the cost of living.
Each category is weighted by the share of household spending it represents, so a price change in a large item (e.g. housing) affects the index more than the same change in a small item (e.g. spices).
Inflation rate (%) = ((CPI in current year − CPI in previous year) / CPI in previous year) × 100. It is the percentage change in the index, not the change in the index itself.
Inflation caused by rising aggregate demand (AD shifting right), pulling the price level up — 'too much spending chasing too few goods'. Shown as a rightward shift of AD along an upward-sloping AS.
Inflation caused by rising costs of production (e.g. wages, imported raw materials, energy) shifting short-run aggregate supply (SRAS) left, pushing the price level up while output falls.
The costs to firms of changing their listed prices when inflation is high — reprinting price lists, updating websites and re-tagging stock.
The time and effort spent minimising cash holdings when inflation is high, e.g. more frequent trips to the bank to move money into interest-bearing accounts.
A self-reinforcing decline: falling prices lead consumers to delay purchases, cutting AD, which pushes prices lower still, raising unemployment and the real value of debt.

Fixed basket ignores changing habits; slow to include new products; hard to separate price rises from quality improvements; one national average hides very different experiences for different households.
Disinflation = inflation rate falling but still positive (prices rising more slowly). Deflation = negative inflation rate (prices actually falling). Do not use them interchangeably.

quickCheck.prompt: In an economy the inflation rate falls from 6% to 4% over a year. This is best described as:
The CPI is described as a WEIGHTED price index. The weights represent:
options: The physical weight of each good in the basket
The share of typical household spending on each category of goods
The inflation rate of each individual good
The total number of goods in the basket
quickCheck.answer: The share of typical household spending on each category of goods

quickCheck.prompt: A country's CPI rises from 120 in year 1 to 126 in year 2. The rate of inflation between the two years is:
A sharp rise in world oil prices increases firms' production costs across the economy and pushes the price level up. This is an example of:
A sustained FALL in the general price level, so that the measured inflation rate is negative, is called:

faq.q: Why do most central banks target about 2% inflation rather than 0%?
faq.a: A small positive target keeps a safety buffer above deflation, which is harder to escape than mild inflation. It also allows 'real' wages to adjust downward without cutting anyone's cash pay: if prices rise 2% and a wage is frozen, the real wage gently falls, helping labour markets clear. Finally, the CPI tends to slightly overstate true inflation, so a 2% measured target is closer to genuine price stability than 0% would be.

faq.q: What is the difference between 'good' and 'bad' deflation?
faq.a: 'Bad' deflation comes from falling aggregate demand — a recessionary collapse in spending that raises unemployment and the real burden of debt, risking a deflationary spiral. 'Good' deflation comes from rising short-run aggregate supply, such as a major productivity or technology gain that lowers costs; here prices fall while output and employment rise. The cause, not the falling price alone, determines whether deflation is harmful.

faq.q: Why is the CPI 'weighted', and what does that change?
faq.a: Households do not spend equally across all goods, so the CPI weights each category by its share of spending. Housing and food carry large weights; a small item like postage carries a tiny one. This means a 10% rise in a heavily weighted category moves the index far more than a 10% rise in a lightly weighted one — the index reflects the price change an average household actually feels, not a simple average of all price tags.

sections.content: A chocolate bar that cost £0.80 a few years ago might cost £1.20 today. Nothing has changed about the chocolate — what has changed is the value of the money used to buy it. That erosion is inflation, and managing it is one of the most important jobs in macroeconomics. Governments and central banks aim for inflation that is both LOW and STABLE, because rapidly rising prices and falling prices each cause serious harm. This lesson shows you how to define, measure and calculate price-level change, diagnose its causes with AD/AS, and evaluate its consequences.

The general price level is the average of prices across an economy. **Inflation** is a sustained rise in that level: money loses purchasing power, so each pound, dollar or euro buys less than before. **Deflation** is the opposite — a sustained fall in the general price level, i.e. a negative inflation rate. Between them sits a term students most often get wrong: **disinflation**, which means the rate of inflation is falling but is still positive. Under disinflation prices are still rising; they are just rising more slowly.

items: **Inflation** — the price level is rising (positive inflation rate, e.g. +4%).
**Disinflation** — the price level is still rising, but the RATE is falling (e.g. inflation slowing from 5% to 3%). This is a fall in the rate, not in prices.
**Deflation** — the price level is actually FALLING (a negative inflation rate, e.g. −1%).
The single most common error here is treating disinflation and deflation as the same thing. They are not: only deflation involves prices falling.

sections.content: Measuring inflation — the Consumer Price Index (CPI)

Inflation is measured by tracking the price of a **basket** of goods and services that a typical household buys. Prices are collected regularly and combined into a **Consumer Price Index (CPI)**. The basket is **weighted**: each category is given a weight equal to its share of household spending, so a price change in a large item such as housing affects the index far more than the same percentage change in a minor item. One year is chosen as the **base year** and its index is set to 100; the index in any other year then shows how prices compare with that base. The **rate of inflation** is the percentage change in the index from one period to the next.

sections.question: A country's consumer price index rises from 125 in year 1 to 131 in year 2. Calculate the rate of inflation between year 1 and year 2. [2]
sections.solution:
**Model answer**

Method — use the percentage-change formula:

Inflation rate = ((CPI in year 2 − CPI in year 1) / CPI in year 1) × 100

Substitute and evaluate:

= ((131 − 125) / 125) × 100
= (6 / 125) × 100
= **4.8%**

The rate of inflation between year 1 and year 2 is **4.8%**.

---
**How our marking engine awards the marks (IB Paper 3 quantitative — POINTS)**

Paper 3 calculations are marked on the POINTS scheme, where **M** marks reward the correct method and **A** marks reward the accurate answer, with full working shown.

- **M1 — method:** correctly applying the percentage-change method — the difference in the index divided by the ORIGINAL (year 1) index, multiplied by 100. Setting up (131 − 125) / 125 × 100 earns this mark even before the arithmetic is finished.
- **A1 — accuracy:** the correct final value, **4.8%**, with the % sign and working shown.
- **Follow-through (ECF):** if you set the method up correctly (M1) but make a small arithmetic slip, our engine still awards the method mark and follows your figure through — you are not penalised twice for one error. Dividing by the wrong base (e.g. by 131 instead of 125), however, loses the method mark, because that is a conceptual error, not a slip.

The classic ways to lose a mark here: quoting only the change in the index (6) instead of the percentage change; dividing by the new index (131) rather than the original (125); or writing the number with no working, which forfeits the method mark if the answer is wrong.

sections.content: Because a household spends different amounts on different categories, the CPI multiplies each category's price index by its spending weight, sums the results, and divides by the total weight. This 'weighted average' is what makes the CPI reflect the price change an average household actually experiences, rather than a simple average of unrelated price tags.

sections.question:
A simplified economy divides household spending into three categories. The table shows each category's weight (its share of spending, out of 100) and its price index for the current year (base year = 100 for every category).

| Category | Weight | Price index (current year) |
|---|---|---|
| Food | 40 | 110 |
| Housing | 35 | 105 |
| Transport | 25 | 120 |

(a) Calculate the weighted CPI for the current year. [3]
(b) State the overall rate of inflation since the base year. [1]
sections.solution:
**Model answer**

**(a)** Method — multiply each category's price index by its weight, sum, then divide by the total weight (100):

Weighted CPI = ((40 × 110) + (35 × 105) + (25 × 120)) / 100
= (4,400 + 3,675 + 3,000) / 100
= 11,075 / 100
= **110.75**

**(b)** Because the base-year index is 100, the current index of 110.75 means prices have risen 10.75% since the base year:

Inflation since base year = ((110.75 − 100) / 100) × 100 = **10.75%**

---
**How our marking engine awards the marks (POINTS)**

- **M1 — method:** weighting each price index by its spending share — i.e. forming (weight × price index) for each category — rather than taking a plain unweighted average of 110, 105 and 120.
- **M1 — method:** dividing the weighted sum by the total weight (100). These two method marks reward the structure of the calculation.
- **A1 — accuracy:** the correct final index, **110.75**, with working shown; the follow-through then carries this figure into part (b), where 10.75% is awarded on your own index value.

Note how the weighting changes the result: a simple average of the three indices would give (110 + 105 + 120) / 3 = 111.7, but because the lightly rising Housing category (weight 35) is large and the fast-rising Transport category (weight 25) is smaller, the correctly weighted figure is lower at 110.75. Reporting the unweighted average is a conceptual error that loses the method marks.

sections.content: This is exactly what the **Practice** button does. Open it, answer a Paper 3 calculation like these in your own working, and submit it — our marking engine grades it on the same POINTS scheme, awarding the M mark for the right method and the A mark for the accurate figure, and applying follow-through so a single arithmetic slip doesn't cascade. The habit that earns full marks: always show the formula, substitute the numbers, and quote the final answer with its unit (% or index points).
The CPI is the standard measure of inflation, but it is an estimate, and examiners reward students who can evaluate its weaknesses rather than treat it as perfect.

items: **The basket is fixed for a period** — but spending habits change continuously, so a basket updated only occasionally can misrepresent what households actually buy today.
**New products enter slowly** — innovative goods (and the price falls that often follow their launch) may not appear in the basket for some time, so genuine cost-of-living changes are missed.
**Quality changes are hard to strip out** — if a phone costs more but is far better, part of the 'price rise' is really improved quality, not pure inflation; separating the two is difficult.

**It is a single national average** — one figure hides very different experiences. Households whose spending is concentrated in fast-rising categories (e.g. energy or rent) face higher effective inflation than the headline rate suggests.

**Substitution is ignored** — when one good rises in price, consumers switch to cheaper alternatives, but a fixed basket does not capture this, tending to overstate the true rise in the cost of living.

sections.content: The causes of inflation: demand-pull and cost-push

Inflation has two broad causes, best shown on an AD/AS diagram. **Demand-pull inflation** originates on the demand side: a rise in aggregate demand pulls the price level up. **Cost-push inflation** originates on the supply side: rising costs of production shift short-run aggregate supply to the left, pushing the price level up. Diagnosing which is at work matters, because the two have different side effects and call for different policy responses.

items: **Demand-pull inflation** — an increase in any component of AD (consumption, investment, government spending or net exports) shifts **AD right**. Along an upward-sloping AS curve, this raises both the price level and real output. It typically occurs when an economy is near full capacity, where extra demand mostly bids up prices rather than raising output.

**Cost-push inflation** — a rise in production costs (higher wages, more expensive imported raw materials, higher energy prices, or a depreciating currency raising import costs) shifts **SRAS left**. This raises the price level while real output FALLS — the uncomfortable combination of rising prices and rising unemployment known as stagflation.

**The key contrast:** demand-pull raises prices AND output together; cost-push raises prices while output falls. On a diagram, demand-pull is a rightward shift of AD; cost-push is a leftward shift of SRAS.

sections.question: An economy is operating close to full employment. The government sharply increases spending and cuts taxes, and consumer and business confidence is high. Using an AD/AS diagram, explain the type of inflation this is likely to cause. [4]
sections.solution:
**Model answer**

*[Diagram: AD/AS with the price level (P) on the vertical axis and real output (Y) on the horizontal axis. An upward-sloping SRAS curve; AD1 shifting rightward to AD2 near the full-employment level of output. Equilibrium moves from point A (P1, Y1) up the SRAS curve to point B (P2, Y2), with P2 > P1.]*

The tax cut, higher government spending and strong confidence all increase components of aggregate demand (government spending G, consumption C and investment I), shifting **AD from AD1 to AD2**. Because the economy is already near full employment, firms cannot easily expand output to meet the extra demand, so the additional spending mainly bids up prices: equilibrium moves from A to B and the price level rises from P1 to P2. This is **demand-pull inflation** — 'too much spending chasing too few goods'. Note that output rises only modestly (Y1 to Y2) because the economy is near capacity; the closer to full employment, the more of the demand increase shows up as higher prices rather than higher output.

---
**How this maps to our markband (explain, 4 marks)**

- Correct **diagram** with axes labelled (price level, real output), AD shifting right and equilibrium moving up the AS curve — and *referred to* in the writing, not just drawn.
- **Terms used accurately** throughout: aggregate demand, its components, demand-pull inflation, full employment.
- **Full explanation of the chain of reasoning**: identifying the AD components affected → the rightward AD shift → why prices rise more than output near capacity. A response that merely labels the shift 'demand-pull' without explaining the mechanism sits in a lower band. Explaining the diagram, not just drawing it, is what our engine rewards.

sections.content: The consequences of high inflation and of deflation
Both high inflation and deflation impose real costs, which is why the objective is low and STABLE inflation rather than zero.

items: **Costs of high inflation:**
* **Loss of purchasing power** — if incomes rise more slowly than prices, real incomes and living standards fall, hitting people on fixed incomes hardest.
* **Uncertainty and lower investment** — when future prices and costs are hard to predict, firms delay investment and long-term planning, weakening growth.
* **Redistribution** — inflation erodes the real value of savings (hurting savers and lenders) while reducing the real value of debt (benefiting borrowers), redistributing wealth arbitrarily.
* **Menu and shoe-leather costs** — firms bear the cost of constantly changing prices, and households waste time and effort trying to protect the value of their money.
* **Loss of international competitiveness** — if inflation is higher than in trading partners, exports become relatively expensive and imports cheaper, worsening the trade balance.
**Costs of deflation:**
* **Deferred consumption** — expecting prices to fall further, households postpone purchases, cutting aggregate demand.
* **Rising real value of debt** — as prices and incomes fall, fixed debts become harder to repay, raising defaults for households and firms.
* **Deflationary spiral** — falling demand leads to lower prices and wages, which cut demand further, entrenching recession.
* **Rising unemployment** — as revenues and profits fall, firms cut output and lay off workers, and interest-rate policy loses traction near zero.

sections.content: When evaluating the effects of inflation, always identify the stakeholders — savers versus borrowers, workers on fixed versus flexible incomes, exporters, and the government as a debtor. Top answers also stress that the RATE and the STABILITY of inflation matter more than its mere existence: moderate, predictable inflation of around 2% is a policy goal, whereas high or volatile inflation is the genuine problem.
Why price stability is a macroeconomic objective

Because both high inflation and deflation are damaging, governments and central banks pursue **price stability** — usually defined as low, positive and predictable inflation, commonly around a 2% target. Stable prices preserve the value of money as a unit of account, so wages, contracts and investment decisions can be planned with confidence. A small positive target also keeps a safety margin above deflation, allows real wages to adjust without cash pay cuts, and offsets the CPI's tendency to slightly overstate true inflation. Price stability is therefore not an end in itself but a foundation for the other macro goals — sustained growth, low unemployment and external balance — because it protects the reliability of the price signals on which the whole economy depends.

items: **Confusing disinflation with deflation** — disinflation means the inflation rate is falling but still positive (prices rising more slowly); deflation means the price level is actually falling (a negative rate). Only deflation involves falling prices.

**Dividing by the wrong base when calculating inflation** — the percentage change divides by the ORIGINAL (earlier) index, not the new one. Dividing by the current index is a conceptual error that loses the method mark.

**Quoting the change in the index instead of the percentage change** — a rise from 125 to 131 is a 6-point change but a 4.8% inflation rate. The inflation rate is always the PERCENTAGE change in the index.
**Treating the CPI as a simple (unweighted) average** — it is weighted by spending shares, so a large category's price change moves the index more than a small category's.

**Mixing up demand-pull and cost-push** — demand-pull shifts AD right and raises prices AND output; cost-push shifts SRAS left and raises prices while output FALLS. Naming the wrong shift on the diagram costs marks.

**Assuming all inflation is bad and all deflation is worse** — the objective is low, stable, positive inflation (about 2%), not zero. Whether deflation harms depends on whether it comes from collapsing demand ('bad') or from rising supply/productivity ('good').
